---
title: "Benchmark: SIMLR vs NED"
subtitle: "Performance on Nonlinear Data with Noise"
format: html
---

## Objective

This benchmark evaluates the advantage of the NED (Nonlinear Encoder, Nonlinear Decoder) model over linear SIMLR when the forward mapping from latent space to modalities is strongly nonlinear. We expect NED to recover the latent signal more accurately.

## Data Generation

We simulate nonlinear forward maps using a mixture of periodic (sine) and polynomial (cubic) transformations:
$$X_i = f_i(U) + \epsilon_i$$
The outcome $y$ also depends nonlinearly on the first two dimensions of $U$:
$$y = \sum \sin(u_{:2}) + \epsilon_y$$

```{python}
import torch
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from pysimlr.simlr import simlr, predict_simlr
from pysimlr.deep import ned_simr, predict_deep
from pysimlr.benchmarks.metrics import calculate_all_metrics

# Parameters
n_samples = 1200
n_train = 800
k = 5
noise = 0.1

torch.manual_seed(42)
u = torch.randn(n_samples, k)

def nonlin_map(z, out_dim):
    w1 = torch.randn(z.shape[1], out_dim)
    w2 = torch.randn(z.shape[1], out_dim)
    return torch.sin(z @ w1) + 0.2 * (z @ w2)**3

x1 = nonlin_map(u, 50) + noise * torch.randn(n_samples, 50)
x2 = nonlin_map(u, 40) + noise * torch.randn(n_samples, 40)
y = torch.sum(torch.sin(u[:, :2]), dim=1, keepdim=True) + 0.05 * torch.randn(n_samples, 1)

# Normalize
def norm(x): return (x - x.mean(0)) / (x.std(0) + 1e-6)
x1, x2 = norm(x1), norm(x2)
y_np = y.numpy()

# Split
train_mats = [x1[:n_train], x2[:n_train]]
test_mats = [x1[n_train:], x2[n_train:]]
y_train, y_test = y_np[:n_train], y_np[n_train:]
u_true_test = u[n_train:]
```

## Results

### SIMLR (Linear)

```{python}
res_simlr = simlr(train_mats, k=k, iterations=100, energy_type="acc")
pred_simlr = predict_simlr(test_mats, res_simlr)
metrics_simlr = calculate_all_metrics(
    pred_simlr['u'], u_true_test, y_test, test_mats, pred_simlr['reconstructions'],
    u_train=res_simlr['u'], y_train=y_train
)
print(f"SIMLR Recovery: {metrics_simlr['recovery']:.4f}")
print(f"SIMLR Test R2:  {metrics_simlr['test_r2']:.4f}")
```

### NED (Deep Nonlinear)

```{python}
res_ned = ned_simr(train_mats, k=k, epochs=150, warmup_epochs=20, verbose=False, device="cpu")
pred_ned = predict_deep(test_mats, res_ned, device="cpu")
metrics_ned = calculate_all_metrics(
    pred_ned['u'], u_true_test, y_test, test_mats, pred_ned['reconstructions'],
    u_train=res_ned['u'], y_train=y_train
)
print(f"NED Recovery: {metrics_ned['recovery']:.4f}")
print(f"NED Test R2:  {metrics_ned['test_r2']:.4f}")
```

## Conclusion

In this nonlinear regime, NED is expected to outperform SIMLR by capturing the complex inverse mapping that a purely linear model cannot represent.
